tweets.text:
$WLD ran from $0.24 to ~$0.59 in three weeks, up ~68% on the week while the market dropped ~10%.

This isn't random altcoin rotation. There's an actual thesis driving it.

It started with @CryptoHayes, the BitMEX co-founder who now runs the investment firm Maelstrom. His firm put out a research note making the case for WLD, and Hayes pushed it hard to his following.

The argument: WLD is the cheapest way to bet on the AI IPO wave. Right now, investors who want exposure to OpenAI or Anthropic have to go through private deals, expensive SPVs, locked-up structures, at valuations in the hundreds of billions.

WLD, by contrast, is liquid, anyone can buy it, and it trades at only a ~$2B market cap. Tiny, by AI standards. Maelstrom's call is $5 by August.

There's a market backdrop helping too. Alts have started moving on their own instead of just following Bitcoin. BTC fell ~6% on June 2, but money kept rotating into risk, and the AI narrative is where most of it landed.
The honest part: this is a story-and-positioning trade, not a product one.

@worldcoin didn't ship anything new. The price is running on attention, which means it lives and dies on risk appetite, and it's already cooling, back around $0.52.

A proxy trade is only as strong as the story behind it. Right now the story is loud. Loud stories fade.
tweets.full_text:
$WLD ran from $0.24 to ~$0.59 in three weeks, up ~68% on the week while the market dropped ~10%.

This isn't random altcoin rotation. There's an actual thesis driving it.

It started with @CryptoHayes, the BitMEX co-founder who now runs the investment firm Maelstrom. His firm put out a research note making the case for WLD, and Hayes pushed it hard to his following.

The argument: WLD is the cheapest way to bet on the AI IPO wave. Right now, investors who want exposure to OpenAI or Anthropic have to go through private deals, expensive SPVs, locked-up structures, at valuations in the hundreds of billions.

WLD, by contrast, is liquid, anyone can buy it, and it trades at only a ~$2B market cap. Tiny, by AI standards. Maelstrom's call is $5 by August.

There's a market backdrop helping too. Alts have started moving on their own instead of just following Bitcoin. BTC fell ~6% on June 2, but money kept rotating into risk, and the AI narrative is where most of it landed.
The honest part: this is a story-and-positioning trade, not a product one.

@worldcoin didn't ship anything new. The price is running on attention, which means it lives and dies on risk appetite, and it's already cooling, back around $0.52.

A proxy trade is only as strong as the story behind it. Right now the story is loud. Loud stories fade.

tweets.text: Curiously enough I did office hours today with a startup that cuts companies' LLM token costs by optimizing requests. They can cut costs by about half, which they split with the customer. So the TAM is a quarter of the model companies' corporate revenue. That's a big TAM!
tweets.quoted_tweet_text: If big companies can't make a net return on their LLM token costs, that doesn't mean it's impossible to. In fact this is exactly what you'd expect to happen with a new technology. Incumbents can't use it well, and are replaced by upstarts who can.
tweets.full_text: Curiously enough I did office hours today with a startup that cuts companies' LLM token costs by optimizing requests. They can cut costs by about half, which they split with the customer. So the TAM is a quarter of the model companies' corporate revenue. That's a big TAM!

tweets.text:
Ugly day across #uranium, #silver, #gold and base metal stocks.

For investors with dry powder, this may prove to be a great buying opportunity.

I'm certainly not considering selling because of a strong U.S. jobs report. The world didn't suddenly start consuming less uranium, silver, copper or other critical commodities today. If anything, long-term demand trends continue to strengthen.

Meanwhile, uranium and silver have been in structural supply deficits for years, with consumption exceeding production. That can continue for a while, until it can't.

Markets often focus on short-term macro headlines, but the underlying fundamentals keep getting stronger.

In my view, today's price action is noise. The real story remains the growing gap between supply and demand, and eventually that gap will have to be reflected in higher commodity prices.
tweets.full_text:
Ugly day across #uranium, #silver, #gold and base metal stocks.

For investors with dry powder, this may prove to be a great buying opportunity.

I'm certainly not considering selling because of a strong U.S. jobs report. The world didn't suddenly start consuming less uranium, silver, copper or other critical commodities today. If anything, long-term demand trends continue to strengthen.

Meanwhile, uranium and silver have been in structural supply deficits for years, with consumption exceeding production. That can continue for a while, until it can't.

Markets often focus on short-term macro headlines, but the underlying fundamentals keep getting stronger.

In my view, today's price action is noise. The real story remains the growing gap between supply and demand, and eventually that gap will have to be reflected in higher commodity prices.

tweets.text: The best startup pitches, like the best books, make claims that are unequivocal. A lot of what I do in office hours at this point in the batch, with demo day approaching, is figure out exactly how much the startup can truly claim.

thread_tweets.text: The best startup pitches, like the best books, make claims that are unequivocal. A lot of what I do in office hours at this point in the batch, with demo day approaching, is figure out exactly how much the startup can truly claim.
thread_tweets.full_text: The best startup pitches, like the best books, make claims that are unequivocal. A lot of what I do in office hours at this point in the batch, with demo day approaching, is figure out exactly how much the startup can truly claim.

thread_tweets.text: One of the most important claims to be able to make is that they're the current leader in their market. It's surprising how many founders aren't consciously aware of this till I interview it out of them.
thread_tweets.full_text: One of the most important claims to be able to make is that they're the current leader in their market. It's surprising how many founders aren't consciously aware of this till I interview it out of them.

thread_tweets.text: This should go without saying, but it's important that startups' claims be not just unequivocal but true as well. That's the point of unequivocal claims: they're definitely either true or false. So (unless you're a pathological liar) they're a way of holding yourself to account.
thread_tweets.full_text: This should go without saying, but it's important that startups' claims be not just unequivocal but true as well. That's the point of unequivocal claims: they're definitely either true or false. So (unless you're a pathological liar) they're a way of holding yourself to account.

tweets.full_text:
The best startup pitches, like the best books, make claims that are unequivocal. A lot of what I do in office hours at this point in the batch, with demo day approaching, is figure out exactly how much the startup can truly claim.

One of the most important claims to be able to make is that they're the current leader in their market. It's surprising how many founders aren't consciously aware of this till I interview it out of them.

This should go without saying, but it's important that startups' claims be not just unequivocal but true as well. That's the point of unequivocal claims: they're definitely either true or false. So (unless you're a pathological liar) they're a way of holding yourself to account.

tweets.text:
GPT-3 level robot foundation models already exist 

It’s just not obvious because GPT-3 was only partially successful most of the time. That’s ok for some knowledge work but not for the work we need robots to do

GPT-5 level intelligence for robots we could see within a year because research compounds

We won’t necessarily have something that looks exactly like the ChatGPT moment for robotics, but the FigureAI livestream was close to that for a lot of people

tweets.text:
T-minus one week until shares of $SPCX start trading. 

I see today’s market slide (Nasdaq down ~3%) as a healthy sign. The biggest risk to the IPO is overly optimistic investors going into the first trade.

Even if the market slides for the next few days, the window is still open, the optimism around the company should be more than enough to power through a soft market leading up to Friday.

tweets.text:
I run an entire AI Investment Team that gives me:

> Morning market reports at 9am SGT across JP / KR open & last night's US market moves 
> Portfolio manager that breaks down latest earnings / news for my holdings
> Research analyst for initiating new names 
> Coverage of Substack / Fintwit's favourite accounts and any new positions they take
> Technical alerts for "BTFD scenarios" - example, large % drops with big volume that tend to provide good entries for the names I like
> Ideation capabilities through ingesting a bunch of content across platforms to build context, so that I can ask it "what should I long for optical networking" and it can answer
tweets.quoted_tweet_text:
i'm obsessed with AI DIY projects.

my favorite one right now is this broccoli farmer in hokkaido, japan using Codex to run his 100-hectare farm

this guy never studied agriculture, never inherited land, started out as a civil servant.

but he wanted his farm to run better, and https://t.co/mEun3Qbz6v

thread_tweets.full_text: @functi0nZer0 i was just debating whether voting weight should be truly private or not; i assume not as this means you could not have prevented this right?

thread_tweets.text:
@functi0nZer0 That'd be my fear; attacker can look at past proposals, see how much votes they may need, buy up the tokens, and then perform hostile takeover. 

Without knowing the total tokens they have, and if the votes are private, no way to even know if you should coordinate or not.

@functi0nZer0 That'd be my fear; attacker can look at past proposals, see how much votes they may need, buy up the tokens, and then perform hostile takeover.

tweets.text:
We need to discuss Business-to-Agents (B2A). Every day, I observe my agents recommending software to me. In this instance, it strongly advocated for sending emails via @resend instead of the conventional Sendgrid, Mailgun, or Amazon SES...

Agents have become significant decision-makers in purchasing software. In most cases, users simply agree, so the question is: how can LLMs have a favorable opinion of my product, ensuring that the agent selects it on behalf of users?

We are moving from a risk averse society to a risk forward society again.

When the govt is short nominal growth, that model makes sense.

That being said, money was too easy to make in the short term, this washout is healthy.
